news_summary: Today's news highlights progress in work-life balance for PhD students, supportive approaches to climate anxiety, and recognition of creative excellence in the gaming industry.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité du jour met en avant les progrès réalisés en matière d'équilibre entre vie professionnelle et vie privée pour les doctorants, les approches visant à aider à gérer l'anxiété climatique, ainsi que la reconnaissance de l'excellence créative dans l'industrie du jeu vidéo.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy destacan los avances en la conciliación entre la vida laboral y personal de los estudiantes de doctorado, las estrategias de apoyo para la ansiedad climática y el reconocimiento de la excelencia creativa en la industria de los videojuegos.

articles.title: The nine-to-five PhD: mere myth or an achievable goal?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 16 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00509-9Can you squeeze your graduate programme into a 40-hour working week? These 13 current and former PhD candidates reveal their top time-management tips.
articles.content:
The nine-to-five PhD is an ever-elusive goal: many candidates aspire to it but few end up achieving such an impressive work–life balance. Nature’s 2025 global PhD survey found that 50% of respondents, who were self-selecting, identified a culture of long work hours at their university. This fuels dissatisfaction, and those who spent more than 60 hours a week on their PhDs were significantly more likely to report that they felt dissatisfied with their doctorates (21%) than were those who spent 30 hours or less (16%).

An analysis of more than 26,000 PhD candidates from 14 universities across the United Kingdom between 2006 and 2017, by online PhD information platform DiscoverPhDs.com, found that one in five fail to get their PhD, mostly owing to candidates leaving their programmes early. Another longitudinal study showed that time pressure was correlated with dropout rates1.

It is not just the candidates who would benefit from making PhD study more sustainable. According to a review published in 2024, PhD holders are more likely to be employed in high-skilled jobs than are undergraduates, and having people with PhDs in teams boosts the productivity of their colleagues who do not have PhDs. PhD holders in industry also help to foster collaboration with academia and investment in research and development, particularly for smaller businesses2.

Here, 13 past and current full-time PhD candidates, who say they spent or are spending an average of 40 hours a week or less on their doctorates, share their advice and observations.

You’re in charge, revel in the flexibility

“Be sure to take time off to make up for overtime: you often don’t have to work the same hours every day or the same hours as other people. And when you’re waiting for feedback from someone, take that as time off! Don’t be afraid to have a flexible schedule.” — Victoria Crozier, a fourth-year ecology PhD candidate at the University of Saskatchewan in Saskatoon, Canada

“Avoid working long hours continuously. My research project in health informatics involves structured phases — particularly during field data collection — which makes time planning more manageable. During proposal writing, I typically worked for three hours in the morning and three hours in the evening. During fieldwork, I usually spend seven hours per day at a health facility, conducting interviews and observating how people use health-management information systems. After each site visit, I spend an extra one to two hours reviewing notes and audio recordings. Although these periods are intensive, they are bounded in time and predictable, which helps prevent work from expanding uncontrollably.” — Bigten Kikoba, a fourth-year health-informatics PhD candidate at the University of Dodoma in Tanzania

“I typically work Monday to Friday from 9.30 a.m. to 7 p.m., but I take half a day off every week (typically on Wednesday morning). This way, I can work the whole week and not reach the weekend exhausted. I am able to organize my work better, because I can concentrate for longer and make sure to finish during my full days. I can also be more relaxed during my time off.” — Claudia Pisanti, a second-year physics PhD candidate at the University of Bologna in Italy

Sounds of science: how music at work can fine-tune your research

“The type of research I do — on a branch of functional analysis known as operator theory — greatly favours flexibility, so it’s easier for me to maintain a nine-to-five schedule than for people in a lot of other fields of study, in particular those that involve being in the laboratory. The only equipment I need are my computer plus some pens and paper or chalk and a board, so I can do research basically anywhere. In fact, I often work from home if I can’t go into the office.” — Julio Enciso, a second-year mathematics PhD candidate at the National Polytechnic Institute in Mexico City

“Many of my experiments span two weeks, so I plan exactly which days and times I will be in the lab before starting. This helps me work out the rest of my schedule, such as slots for meetings, without overstretching myself. The same principle applies to computer-based work: if I have multiple experiments planned, I intentionally lighten the load of tasks such as writing. Conversely, if I have a major deadline approaching, I avoid scheduling experiments during that period. There will be occasions when you need to push yourself hard, and others when things slow down and you have more time to breathe.” — Sarah McPhedran, a third-year immunology PhD candidate at the Deeley Research Centre in Victoria, Canada

You might work 40 hours a week, but not necessarily nine-to-five

“The eight-hour day and 40-hour week was created for factory work in which every day was the same, not academic work, for which each day can look wildly different. I don’t even remotely keep to a nine-to-five schedule. I’ve never been a morning person; my body prefers getting up later and working later. During my master’s, I tried to work nine-to-five, but I found myself wasting time and sitting at my desk pretending to work because I was always so tired.” — Victoria Crozier

“Fieldwork tends to defy normal working hours, especially when it comes to multi-day trips that you want to make as cost-effective as possible. Also, the conferences and science fairs at which you present your work do not always follow a standard schedule. Sometimes working outside the nine-to-five routine is a positive thing, especially when researching a fresh, interesting topic: I read paper upon paper out of sheer curiosity late into the night.” — Kateřina Bezányiová, a second-year zoology PhD candidate at Charles University in Prague

Know your limits

“Know when to stop working: avoid answering non-emergency e-mails late in the evening or at weekends. Instead, set aside dedicated time for these admin tasks. However, if there is something with a distant deadline and you have energy to spare, then you might as well start working on it. Making use of the half hour it takes to get the result of a PCR experiment in the lab can save you sleepless nights of feverish work before a deadline.” — Kateřina Bezányiová

“I like referring to my department’s policies regarding working hours. My department has explicit statements recommending a 40-hour work week. So, even if my peers are working more hours, I know that I am performing according to the department’s expectations.” — Karen Arevalo, a fifth-year kidney-cancer-biology PhD candidate at the University of Toronto in Canada

“Developing the ability to say no is an essential skill in graduate school. Setting firm boundaries not only protects your time and mental health, but also often earns respect from others. Consistently saying yes can lead to being overextended or taken advantage of.” — Sarah McPhedran

Be organized

“It all boils down to time management. If you walk into the lab without a plan, you will procrastinate and eventually find yourself working overtime to catch up. If you know something will take you the whole week to finish, plan accordingly. I use a Gantt chart to, as accurately as possible, plan out my PhD and give myself realistic deadlines. It helps me maintain progress by feeling like I’m ticking achievable things off a list.” — Luke Nel, a second-year palaeoecology candidate at Nelson Mandela University in Gqeberha, South Africa

“Set small milestones throughout your project. Finding a dedicated place at the university to carry out non-experimental tasks, such as writing and reviewing, helped me to cut down my hours. It also helped me to avoid taking work home.” — Leo Maia do Amaral, who holds a PhD in engineering and materials science from the University of São Paulo, Brazil

“Plan right from the start. Look at your submission deadlines and plan backwards from those. Every day, plan to write a certain amount of words, and try to stick to that figure.” — Sandra Kiessling, who holds a PhD in engineering from the University of Staffordshire in Stoke-on-Trent, UK

How to get the best night’s sleep: what the science says

“Know your priorities and how long it takes, realistically, for you to finish your tasks. I often work with samples that easily degrade or get contaminated and so I need to carefully plan my work beforehand. I know what my top priority is and what it would be great to get done. But I’m prepared to wait if something takes longer than expected.” — Kateřina Bezányiová

“It might take time to optimize, but figure out what schedule works best for you. I do my most intensive work in the morning and early afternoon. After about 2 p.m., when I start to feel burnt out, I rest. I often return to work in the evening and use those evenings when I’m tired for mindless administrative work or routine data analysis.” — Sarah McPhedran
articles.reasoning: The article highlights practical strategies and cultural shifts towards sustainable work-life balance for PhD candidates, addressing a significant issue affecting many students worldwide. It provides detailed, actionable advice from diverse candidates, promoting mental health and productivity in academia, which has broad societal benefits through improved education and research outcomes.
articles.personality_title: PhD students share tips for keeping work within 40 hours a week
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many PhD students work long hours, often more than 60 per week, which can cause stress and dissatisfaction. Studies show that working too much increases the chance of dropping out, while PhD holders contribute important skills to jobs and research.

**What happened** – Thirteen current and former PhD students who manage to work about 40 hours a week shared their advice. They talked about using flexible schedules, planning work carefully, setting boundaries, and balancing busy and slow periods. For example, some avoid working every day at the same time, while others use charts to plan their progress.

**Impact** – This advice shows that a more balanced PhD life is possible. It challenges the idea that long hours are necessary for success. By managing time well, students can protect their health and finish their studies without burning out. This also benefits workplaces, as happier PhD holders bring better skills and teamwork.

**What's next step** – Sharing these strategies could encourage more students and universities to support healthier working habits. Departments might adopt clearer rules about working hours, and students can learn to set limits and plan better. Over time, this could improve PhD completion rates and mental well-being.

**One-sentence takeaway** – PhD students can achieve a healthy work-life balance by using flexible schedules, good planning, and clear boundaries to keep their workweek around 40 hours.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des doctorants partagent leurs astuces pour limiter leur travail à 40 heures par semaine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de doctorants travaillent plus de 60 heures par semaine, ce qui cause stress et insatisfaction. Des études montrent que ces longues heures augmentent le risque d’abandon, alors que les titulaires de doctorat apportent des compétences importantes au travail et à la recherche.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Treize doctorants actuels ou anciens, qui travaillent environ 40 heures par semaine, ont partagé leurs conseils. Ils parlent d’utiliser des horaires flexibles, de bien planifier leur travail, de poser des limites et d’équilibrer les périodes intenses et calmes. Par exemple, certains évitent de travailler aux mêmes heures chaque jour, d’autres utilisent des tableaux pour organiser leurs tâches.

**Impact** – Ces conseils montrent qu’un équilibre de vie plus sain est possible pendant un doctorat. Ils remettent en cause l’idée que de longues heures sont indispensables pour réussir. En gérant bien leur temps, les étudiants protègent leur santé et peuvent terminer leurs études sans épuisement. Cela profite aussi aux lieux de travail, car des doctorants heureux apportent de meilleures compétences et un meilleur travail d’équipe.

**Prochaine étape** – Diffuser ces stratégies pourrait encourager plus d’étudiants et d’universités à soutenir des habitudes de travail plus saines. Les départements pourraient adopter des règles claires sur les heures de travail, et les étudiants apprendre à poser des limites et à mieux planifier. Avec le temps, cela pourrait améliorer les taux de réussite et le bien-être mental.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Les doctorants peuvent trouver un bon équilibre entre travail et vie personnelle en utilisant des horaires flexibles, une bonne organisation et des limites claires pour rester autour de 40 heures par semaine.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudiantes de doctorado comparten consejos para limitar su trabajo a 40 horas semanales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos estudiantes de doctorado trabajan más de 60 horas a la semana, lo que genera estrés e insatisfacción. Estudios muestran que estas largas horas aumentan el riesgo de abandono, mientras que los doctores aportan habilidades valiosas al trabajo y a la investigación.

**Qué pasó** – Trece estudiantes actuales y antiguos de doctorado que trabajan unas 40 horas semanales compartieron sus consejos. Hablaron de usar horarios flexibles, planificar bien el trabajo, poner límites y equilibrar momentos intensos y tranquilos. Por ejemplo, algunos evitan trabajar siempre a la misma hora, y otros usan gráficos para organizar sus tareas.

**Impacto** – Estos consejos muestran que un mejor equilibrio entre vida y estudio es posible durante un doctorado. Cuestionan la idea de que hay que trabajar muchas horas para tener éxito. Al gestionar bien su tiempo, los estudiantes cuidan su salud y pueden terminar sus estudios sin agotarse. Esto también beneficia al trabajo en equipo y la productividad.

**Próximo paso** – Compartir estas estrategias puede ayudar a que más estudiantes y universidades apoyen hábitos de trabajo más saludables. Los departamentos podrían establecer reglas claras sobre las horas de trabajo, y los estudiantes aprender a poner límites y planificar mejor. Con el tiempo, esto podría mejorar las tasas de graduación y la salud mental.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los estudiantes de doctorado pueden lograr un equilibrio saludable entre trabajo y vida personal usando horarios flexibles, buena planificación y límites claros para mantener unas 40 horas semanales.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy, softly lit study nook with an organized desk featuring a gently glowing laptop, scattered research notes, and a steaming cup of tea, framed by a window showing a calm evening sky, symbolizing balanced and flexible PhD work hours and mindful time management.

articles.title: Ask a Climate Therapist: Why should I plan for my future when I feel we don’t have one?
articles.content:
Dear Leslie,

I’m a young adult trying to figure out what I’m doing with my life. It feels really hard to plan for my future given the uncertainty climate change produces, and sometimes I feel like my degree, which I’m passionate about, will be useless “when the apocalypse comes.” How can I plan well for my future when I don’t know what the world will look like in 10 years, let alone 50?

— Scared Student

Dear Scared Student,

You’re asking one of the most significant questions of your generation, and it’s one that I’m hearing more and more from young people who are tuned into the rapid shifts occurring on our planet. Anyone who claims to know exactly what our changing world will look like in 50 years is deceiving themselves. It takes courage to sit with that uncertainty rather than push it away, so let’s start there: Give yourself credit for remaining aware of both the realities and the unknowns.

That said, the word “apocalypse” doesn’t leave a whole lot of room for possibility. It’s hard to believe that anything matters if the end of times is a foregone conclusion. I think it’s worth breaking that down into two inquiries: what might actually happen, and how the uncertainty is affecting your ability to move forward.

Ask a Climate Therapist tackles your questions about how to navigate the emotional side of climate change, with leading climate-aware therapist Leslie Davenport. Have a question? tackles your questions about how to navigate the emotional side of climate change, with leading climate-aware therapist Leslie Davenport. Have a question? Ask it here

Serious disruptions are taking place already and are essentially a guarantee — including in whatever field you’re training for. I don’t want to minimize the real fear underlying your question. Planning for a future that feels unstable is a genuine challenge. Let yourself feel the weight of that before reaching for solutions. Whatever our future does hold, we can be certain it will look different from the world we know today — perhaps in some wonderful ways, and likely in some bad. Feeling the loss is part of staying whole — neither turning away from what worries you, nor losing sight of what you can still shape.

But collapsing all of our possible futures into a single worst-case scenario tends to make us freeze, and freezing helps neither us nor the communities and ecosystems depending on our action.

Unfreezing, then, is not about pretending that everything will be fine: It’s about developing a skillful agility that allows you to shift as circumstances change.

The skills, relationships, ways of thinking, and capacity for meaning-making that you cultivate when pursuing work you’re passionate about can translate into a wide range of scenarios. They’re not locked inside a single job title. The question isn’t whether your degree will survive in the future; it’s how you can show up with depth and flexibility and continue to seek out the next useful contribution, both personally and professionally.

It can help to shift from long-range certainty-seeking to values-based navigation. Instead of asking, “Will this matter in 50 years?” try asking, “What matters to me now, and how can I build a life that honors that?” This is a core insight from acceptance-based approaches to anxiety. When we loosen our grip on specific outcomes and orient ourselves toward what we value, we can become more resilient and sustain our motivation. Values travel with us, and they’re what allow us to keep pivoting as circumstances change.

No one can plan for a fixed future. Such a thing doesn’t exist. You’re developing yourself and your work in a very dynamic world that will challenge you to use your creative power as you pursue your goals. Your passion isn’t a liability. Today and in the future, whatever transpires, the paramount need is for people who care deeply.

With you in this,

Leslie
articles.reasoning: The article provides meaningful emotional support and guidance for young people struggling with climate anxiety, encouraging resilience and adaptive planning despite uncertainty. It addresses a widespread psychological challenge related to climate change, offering practical mental health strategies that can empower individuals to navigate an unstable future. While it does not report a specific breakthrough or policy, it has broad positive real-world impact by fostering hope and mental well-being in the face of climate crisis.
articles.personality_title: How to Plan Your Future When Climate Change Feels Uncertain
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many young people today worry about climate change and how it will affect their future. This uncertainty makes it hard for them to plan their careers or life goals.

**What happened** – A climate therapist named Leslie Davenport answered a letter from a young adult who feels scared about planning for the future because of climate change. Leslie explained that while the future is uncertain, it’s important to accept that and focus on what really matters now. She encouraged young people to use their passions and values to stay flexible and strong.

**Impact** – This advice helps young people feel less frozen by fear and more able to adapt as the world changes. It shows that skills and values can guide them through many possible futures, not just one fixed outcome. This kind of emotional support is important because it helps people stay motivated and hopeful even when the future feels unclear.

**What’s next step** – People can start focusing on what they care about most today and build their lives around those values. This way, they can keep adjusting their plans as the world changes. Therapists and educators might use these ideas to help more young people deal with climate anxiety.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Accepting uncertainty and focusing on personal values can help young people plan their futures despite climate change worries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment planifier son avenir face à l’incertitude du changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de jeunes s’inquiètent du changement climatique et de son impact sur leur avenir. Cette incertitude rend difficile la planification de leur carrière ou de leurs projets de vie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une thérapeute spécialisée dans le climat, Leslie Davenport, a répondu à une lettre d’un jeune qui a peur de planifier son avenir à cause du changement climatique. Leslie a expliqué qu’il est important d’accepter l’incertitude et de se concentrer sur ce qui compte vraiment aujourd’hui. Elle a encouragé les jeunes à utiliser leurs passions et leurs valeurs pour rester flexibles et forts.

**Impact** – Ce conseil aide les jeunes à ne pas rester paralysés par la peur, mais à s’adapter aux changements du monde. Il montre que les compétences et les valeurs peuvent les guider dans de nombreux futurs possibles, pas seulement un seul. Ce soutien émotionnel est important car il aide à garder la motivation et l’espoir même quand l’avenir semble incertain.

**Prochaine étape** – Les personnes peuvent commencer à se concentrer sur ce qui leur tient à cœur aujourd’hui et construire leur vie autour de ces valeurs. Ainsi, elles pourront ajuster leurs plans au fur et à mesure des changements. Les thérapeutes et les éducateurs pourraient utiliser ces idées pour aider davantage de jeunes à gérer l’anxiété liée au climat.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Accepter l’incertitude et se concentrer sur ses valeurs personnelles aide les jeunes à planifier leur avenir malgré les inquiétudes liées au changement climatique.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo planificar tu futuro cuando el cambio climático genera incertidumbre
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos jóvenes hoy en día se preocupan por el cambio climático y cómo afectará su futuro. Esta incertidumbre dificulta planear sus carreras o metas de vida.

**Qué pasó** – Una terapeuta especializada en clima, Leslie Davenport, respondió a una carta de un joven que tiene miedo de planificar su futuro debido al cambio climático. Leslie explicó que, aunque el futuro es incierto, es importante aceptarlo y enfocarse en lo que realmente importa ahora. Animó a los jóvenes a usar sus pasiones y valores para mantenerse flexibles y fuertes.

**Impacto** – Este consejo ayuda a los jóvenes a no quedar paralizados por el miedo y a adaptarse a medida que el mundo cambia. Muestra que las habilidades y valores pueden guiarlos en muchos futuros posibles, no solo en uno fijo. Este apoyo emocional es importante porque ayuda a mantener la motivación y la esperanza incluso cuando el futuro parece incierto.

**Próximo paso** – Las personas pueden comenzar a enfocarse en lo que más les importa hoy y construir sus vidas alrededor de esos valores. Así, podrán ajustar sus planes a medida que el mundo cambie. Los terapeutas y educadores podrían usar estas ideas para ayudar a más jóvenes a manejar la ansiedad climática.

**Frase clave** – Aceptar la incertidumbre y enfocarse en los valores personales puede ayudar a los jóvenes a planificar su futuro a pesar de las preocupaciones por el cambio climático.
articles.image_prompt: A young sapling growing resiliently from a cracked earth that gently transitions into a vibrant, shifting landscape of changing seasons, with softly glowing lanterns floating upward symbolizing hope, adaptability, and the courage to embrace an uncertain future.

articles.title: Clair Obscur takes top Bafta Games Award but misses out on clean sweep
articles.summary: The French role-playing game is crowned at another ceremony - but takes just a handful of awards home.
articles.content: He went on to say the studio had received messages from "so many people who have been helped by the game", which deals with emotional topics such as grief and loss.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a video game that addresses emotional topics like grief and loss, which has positively impacted many players. This represents a meaningful cultural contribution with emotional and mental health benefits for a broad audience.
articles.personality_title: French game Clair Obscur wins top Bafta Games Award for its emotional story
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Clair Obscur is a French role-playing video game that explores deep emotional topics like grief and loss. Games that focus on feelings and mental health are becoming more common, helping players understand and cope with difficult experiences.

**What happened** – Clair Obscur won the top award at the Bafta Games ceremony. Although it did not win many awards overall, it was recognized for its powerful storytelling and the way it connects with players on an emotional level. The game’s creators shared that many players have told them how the game helped them.

**Impact** – This award shows that video games can be more than just entertainment; they can also support emotional healing and mental health. Clair Obscur stands out because it talks about sensitive topics in a way that reaches and helps a wide audience.

**What's next step** – The recognition might encourage more game makers to create stories that explore important feelings and mental health issues. It could also lead to more players trying games that offer emotional support.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Clair Obscur’s Bafta win highlights how video games can thoughtfully address grief and loss, offering comfort to many players.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le jeu français Clair Obscur remporte le prix principal aux Bafta Games grâce à son histoire émouvante
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Clair Obscur est un jeu de rôle français qui aborde des sujets émotionnels profonds comme le deuil et la perte. Les jeux qui traitent des sentiments et de la santé mentale deviennent plus courants, aidant les joueurs à comprendre et gérer des expériences difficiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Clair Obscur a remporté le prix principal lors de la cérémonie des Bafta Games. Bien qu’il n’ait pas gagné beaucoup de prix au total, il a été reconnu pour son récit puissant et sa connexion émotionnelle avec les joueurs. Les créateurs du jeu ont partagé que de nombreux joueurs leur ont dit que le jeu les avait aidés.

**Impact** – Ce prix montre que les jeux vidéo peuvent être plus que du divertissement ; ils peuvent aussi soutenir la guérison émotionnelle et la santé mentale. Clair Obscur se distingue parce qu’il parle de sujets sensibles d’une manière qui touche et aide un large public.

**Prochaine étape** – Cette reconnaissance pourrait encourager d’autres créateurs à réaliser des histoires qui explorent des sentiments importants et des problèmes de santé mentale. Cela pourrait aussi inciter plus de joueurs à essayer des jeux offrant un soutien émotionnel.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La victoire de Clair Obscur aux Bafta montre comment les jeux vidéo peuvent aborder avec sensibilité le deuil et la perte, apportant du réconfort à de nombreux joueurs.
articles.personality_title_es: El juego francés Clair Obscur gana el premio principal de Bafta Games por su historia emocional
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Clair Obscur es un juego de rol francés que trata temas emocionales profundos como el duelo y la pérdida. Los juegos que abordan sentimientos y salud mental son cada vez más comunes y ayudan a los jugadores a entender y afrontar experiencias difíciles.

**Qué pasó** – Clair Obscur ganó el premio principal en la ceremonia de Bafta Games. Aunque no ganó muchos premios en total, fue reconocido por su poderosa narrativa y la forma en que conecta emocionalmente con los jugadores. Los creadores del juego dijeron que muchas personas les han contado que el juego les ayudó.

**Impacto** – Este premio muestra que los videojuegos pueden ser más que entretenimiento; también pueden apoyar la sanación emocional y la salud mental. Clair Obscur destaca porque habla de temas sensibles de una manera que llega y ayuda a muchas personas.

**Próximo paso** – Este reconocimiento podría motivar a más desarrolladores a crear historias que exploren sentimientos importantes y problemas de salud mental. También podría hacer que más jugadores prueben juegos que ofrecen apoyo emocional.

**Resumen en una frase** – La victoria de Clair Obscur en Bafta resalta cómo los videojuegos pueden abordar con cuidado el duelo y la pérdida, ofreciendo consuelo a muchos jugadores.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a softly glowing lantern floating gently above a serene, shadowed landscape, symbolizing hope and emotional healing, with subtle abstract shapes resembling intertwined hands and hearts beneath it, all rendered in gentle, natural earth tones.
